# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Style]**
**Q: Define a hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^n$. Give the equation of a hyperplane in 2D and 3D with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **hyperplane** in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is an affine subspace of dimension $n-1$ defined as the set of all points $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ satisfying:

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the non-zero normal vector and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias.

- **In 2D:** A hyperplane is a line, e.g., $2x_1 + 3x_2 - 6 = 0$.
- **In 3D:** A hyperplane is a plane, e.g., $x_1 + x_2 + x_3 - 1 = 0$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Style]**
**Q: What is a halfspace? Differentiate between positive and negative halfspaces with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **halfspace** is one of the two regions into which a hyperplane divides $\mathbb{R}^n$.

- **Positive halfspace:** $\mathcal{H}^+ = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n : \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b \geq 0 \}$
- **Negative halfspace:** $\mathcal{H}^- = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n : \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b \leq 0 \}$

**Example:** For the line $x_1 + x_2 - 1 = 0$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$:
- $\mathcal{H}^+ = \{ (x_1, x_2) : x_1 + x_2 \geq 1 \}$
- $\mathcal{H}^- = \{ (x_1, x_2) : x_1 + x_2 \leq 1 \}$

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> (1) Students often forget the **absolute value** in the distance formula ŌĆö this loses 2 marks immediately.
> (2) Confusing **signed** vs **unsigned** distance ŌĆö examiners check this carefully.
> (3) Forgetting to **normalize by $\| \mathbf{w} \|$** in the distance formula.
> (4) Mixing up which side is positive halfspace ŌĆö depends on the **sign convention of $\mathbf{w}$**.
> (5) Failing to **simplify the normal vector** before computing distance (this can still give correct answer but wastes time).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 1

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a)** Define a hyperplane and a halfspace. Derive the equation of a hyperplane passing through a given point $\mathbf{p}$ with a given normal vector $\mathbf{w}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Find the equation of the hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^3$ passing through the points $P_1 = (1, 2, 3)$, $P_2 = (4, 0, 1)$, $P_3 = (2, 5, 0)$. Hence determine whether the origin lies in the positive or negative halfspace and compute the distance from the origin to the hyperplane. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **hyperplane** in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is the set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ for some non-zero $\mathbf{w}$ and scalar $b$. A **halfspace** is the set of points lying on one side of the hyperplane.

**Derivation:**

Let $\mathbf{p}$ be a known point on the hyperplane and $\mathbf{w}$ be the normal. For any other point $\mathbf{x}$ on the hyperplane, the vector $\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{p}$ lies in the hyperplane and is therefore orthogonal to $\mathbf{w}$:

$$\mathbf{w}^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{p}) = 0$$

Expanding:

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{p} = 0$$

Defining $b = -\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{p}$:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Compute two direction vectors lying in the plane:

$$\mathbf{v}_1 = P_2 - P_1 = (3, -2, -2)$$
$$\mathbf{v}_2 = P_3 - P_1 = (1, 3, -3)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the normal via cross product:

$$\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{v}_1 \times \mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{vmatrix} \mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\ 3 & -2 & -2 \\ 1 & 3 & -3 \end{vmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{i}((-2)(-3) - (-2)(3)) - \mathbf{j}((3)(-3) - (-2)(1)) + \mathbf{k}((3)(3) - (-2)(1))$$

$$\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{i}(6 + 6) - \mathbf{j}(-9 + 2) + \mathbf{k}(9 + 2)$$

$$\mathbf{w} = (12, 7, 11)$$

**Step 3:** Compute $b$ using $P_1$:

$$b = -\mathbf{w}^T P_1 = -(12 \cdot 1 + 7 \cdot 2 + 11 \cdot 3) = -(12 + 14 + 33) = -59$$

**Step 4:** Hyperplane equation:

$$12 x_1 + 7 x_2 + 11 x_3 - 59 = 0$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate at origin:

$$f(0, 0, 0) = -59 < 0$$

Origin lies in the **negative halfspace** $\mathcal{H}^-$.

**Step 6:** Distance from origin:

$$\| \mathbf{w} \| = \sqrt{12^2 + 7^2 + 11^2} = \sqrt{144 + 49 + 121} = \sqrt{314}$$

$$d = \frac{\vert -59 \vert}{\sqrt{314}} = \frac{59}{\sqrt{314}} \approx \frac{59}{17.72} \approx 3.33$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of hyperplanes with the equation $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$. Discuss the geometric significance of $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$ in 2D and 3D. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the formula for the perpendicular distance from a point $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$. For the line $3x_1 + 4x_2 - 12 = 0$, compute the distance of the point $(1, 1)$ from the line and state which halfspace it belongs to. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The equation $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ defines a hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

- **$\mathbf{w}$ (normal vector):** It is perpendicular to the hyperplane and points into the positive halfspace. Its direction determines the orientation of the hyperplane.
- **$b$ (bias):** It shifts the hyperplane away from the origin. When $b = 0$, the hyperplane passes through the origin and becomes a linear subspace.

**In 2D:** The hyperplane reduces to a line $w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = 0$, where $\mathbf{w}$ is perpendicular to the line.

**In 3D:** The hyperplane is a plane $w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_3 x_3 + b = 0$, where $\mathbf{w}$ is the normal to the plane.

Doubling $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$ gives the same hyperplane (the equation is invariant to positive scaling).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Derivation:**

Let $\mathbf{x}^*$ be the closest point on the hyperplane to $\mathbf{x}_0$. The vector from $\mathbf{x}^*$ to $\mathbf{x}_0$ is parallel to $\mathbf{w}$:

$$\mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}^* = \lambda \mathbf{w}$$

So $\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{x}_0 - \lambda \mathbf{w}$. Substituting into the hyperplane equation:

$$\mathbf{w}^T (\mathbf{x}_0 - \lambda \mathbf{w}) + b = 0$$

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 - \lambda \| \mathbf{w} \|^2 + b = 0$$

$$\lambda = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b}{\| \mathbf{w} \|^2}$$

The distance is $\| \lambda \mathbf{w} \| = \vert \lambda \vert \| \mathbf{w} \|$:

$$d = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert}{\| \mathbf{w} \|}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

For line $3x_1 + 4x_2 - 12 = 0$ and point $(1, 1)$:

$$\| \mathbf{w} \| = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b = 3(1) + 4(1) - 12 = -5$$

$$d = \frac{\vert -5 \vert}{5} = 1$$

Since $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b = -5 < 0$, the point $(1, 1)$ lies in the **negative halfspace** $\mathcal{H}^-$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap ŌĆö Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- **Definition:** A hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is an affine subspace of dimension $n-1$ defined by $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$.
- **Normal vector $\mathbf{w}$:** Always perpendicular to the hyperplane; points toward $\mathcal{H}^+$.
- **Bias $b$:** Translates the hyperplane; $b = 0$ means it passes through the origin.
- **Positive halfspace $\mathcal{H}^+$:** Points satisfying $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b \geq 0$.
- **Negative halfspace $\mathcal{H}^-$:** Points satisfying $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b \leq 0$.
- **Distance formula:** $d = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert}{\| \mathbf{w} \|}$ ŌĆö **always** uses absolute value.
- **Signed distance:** No absolute value; sign tells you which halfspace.
- **Invariance:** Scaling $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ by any positive scalar $\alpha$ gives the same hyperplane.
- **Classifier:** $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)$.
- **SVM margin:** Distance between parallel supporting hyperplanes is $\dfrac{2}{\| \mathbf{w} \|}$.
- **Convexity:** Both halfspaces are convex sets; intersection of halfspaces = convex polytope.
- **Dimensionality rule:** In $\mathbb{R}^n$, hyperplane has dimension $n-1$.
- **In $\mathbb{R}^2$:** hyperplane is a line; in $\mathbb{R}^3$: it is a plane; in $\mathbb{R}^n$: abstract flat $(n-1)$-subspace.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Confusing dot product notation $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$ with matrix multiplication; always remember $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$ is a scalar in this context.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eigenvalues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT722 - DATA SCIENCE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Eigenvalues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **eigenvalue** (German: *eigen* = "own, characteristic") of a square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is a scalar $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ (or $\mathbb{C}$) that satisfies the fundamental **eigenvalue equation**:

$$A v = \lambda v$$

where $v \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is a **non-zero** column vector called the **eigenvector** corresponding to $\lambda$. Together, the pair $(\lambda, v)$ is referred to as the **eigenpair** of the matrix $A$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEITT722 - Module 2):** Eigenvalues form the mathematical backbone of dimensionality reduction techniques (PCA), spectral clustering, Google's PageRank algorithm, and vibration analysis in mechanical structures. Mastering the characteristic polynomial is a mandatory learning outcome.

### 1.2 Reformulation: The Null-Space Condition

Rearranging $Av = \lambda v$ by moving $\lambda v$ to the left-hand side:

$$(A - \lambda I) v = 0$$

For a **non-trivial** eigenvector $v \neq \mathbf{0}$ to exist, the matrix $(A - \lambda I)$ must be **singular** (i.e., rank-deficient). This gives the celebrated **characteristic condition**:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

This determinant is a polynomial in $\lambda$ of degree $n$ called the **characteristic polynomial** $p(\lambda)$, whose $n$ roots (counted with multiplicity, over $\mathbb{C}$) are the eigenvalues of $A$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Stretching Rubber Sheet

Imagine a square rubber sheet stretched over a frame. Now, apply a **linear transformation** $A$ (e.g., shear, scaling, rotation) to this sheet. Most vectors (arrows drawn on the sheet) will change both their **length** AND their **direction**.

However, there exist some very special "magic directions" along which vectors are only **stretched, squished, or flipped** ŌĆö but their **direction does not change**. The factor by which they are stretched is precisely the **eigenvalue** $\lambda$, and the direction itself is the **eigenvector** $v$.

| Transformation | Eigenvalue Meaning | Intuition |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| $\lambda > 1$ | Stretching in direction $v$ | Arrow elongates |
| $0 < \lambda < 1$ | Shrinking in direction $v$ | Arrow contracts |
| $\lambda = 0$ | Collapse (rank-deficient matrix) | Arrow flattened to origin |
| $\lambda < 0$ | Flip + scaling | Arrow points opposite direction |
| $\vert \lambda \vert = 1$ | Pure rotation/reflection | Length preserved, direction may reverse |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "non-zero" for eigenvectors?** If $v = \mathbf{0}$ is allowed, then $A \mathbf{0} = \lambda \mathbf{0}$ is satisfied for **every** $\lambda$, making the concept meaningless. Hence, the non-zero constraint is fundamental.

### 1.4 Notation Conventions Used in KTU Boards

- $\lambda_i$ or $\mu_i$ ŌĆö the $i$-th eigenvalue
- $v_i$ or $x_i$ ŌĆö the eigenvector corresponding to $\lambda_i$
- $\sigma_i$ ŌĆö singular value (related but uses $A^TA$, not $A$)
- $\rho(A) = \max_i \vert \lambda_i \vert$ ŌĆö the **spectral radius**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Logical Steps of Eigenvalue Computation

The KTU board expects students to follow a strict, deterministic algorithm. Master these six steps:

1. **Form the shifted matrix** $A - \lambda I$ by subtracting $\lambda$ from every diagonal entry of $A$, leaving the off-diagonal entries untouched.
2. **Compute the determinant** $\det(A - \lambda I)$ using cofactor expansion (Laplace), row-reduction, or Sarrus' rule (for $2 \times 2$ and $3 \times 3$).
3. **Set the determinant to zero** $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ to obtain the characteristic polynomial $p(\lambda)$.
4. **Solve the polynomial equation** $p(\lambda) = 0$ using factoring, the rational root theorem, the quadratic formula, or numerical methods (QR, power iteration).
5. **Substitute each root** $\lambda_i$ back into $(A - \lambda_i I)v = 0$ and row-reduce the augmented matrix.
6. **Read off the null space** to obtain the eigenvector $v_i$ (always normalize for uniqueness).

### 2.2 Existence, Multiplicity, and Algebraic vs. Geometric Multiplicity

- **Existence Theorem**: Every $n \times n$ matrix has exactly $n$ eigenvalues in $\mathbb{C}$ (counted with algebraic multiplicity), guaranteed by the **Fundamental Theorem of Algebra**.
- **Algebraic Multiplicity** $m_a(\lambda_i)$: How many times $\lambda_i$ appears as a root of $p(\lambda)$.
- **Geometric Multiplicity** $m_g(\lambda_i) = \dim \text{Null}(A - \lambda_i I)$: Dimension of the eigenspace.
- **CayleyŌĆōHamilton Inequality**: $1 \leq m_g(\lambda_i) \leq m_a(\lambda_i) \leq n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap**: A matrix can be defective ($m_g < m_a$). In such cases, $A$ is **not diagonalizable**. The classic example is $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ with $\lambda = 2$ (algebraic multiplicity 2, geometric multiplicity 1).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Use Case |
|:--:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Eigenvalue equation | $A v = \lambda v$ | Defining the eigenpair |
| 2 | Characteristic polynomial | $p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)$ | Finding all eigenvalues |
| 3 | Sum of eigenvalues (Trace) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \text{tr}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{ii}$ | Quick verification on board |
| 4 | Product of eigenvalues (Det) | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \det(A)$ | Checking determinant parity |
| 5 | Eigenvalues of $A^k$ | $\lambda_i(A^k) = [\lambda_i(A)]^k$ | Markov chain steady states |
| 6 | Eigenvalues of $A^{-1}$ | $\lambda_i(A^{-1}) = 1 / \lambda_i(A)$ (if $\det A \neq 0$) | Inverse problems, stability |
| 7 | Eigenvalues of $A + cI$ | $\lambda_i(A + cI) = \lambda_i(A) + c$ | Centering data matrices in PCA |
| 8 | Spectral radius | $\rho(A) = \max_i \vert \lambda_i \vert$ | Convergence of $A^k$ as $k \to \infty$ |
| 9 | Spectral theorem (Symmetric) | $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$, with $Q$ orthogonal | PCA guarantees real eigenvalues |
| 10 | Gershgorin disc bound | $\vert \lambda - a_{ii} \vert \leq \sum_{j \neq i} \vert a_{ij} \vert$ | Localizing eigenvalues numerically |

> **CRITICAL NOTATION NOTE:** In all subsequent KTU board answers, the determinant notation $\vert A - \lambda I \vert$ is written in LaTeX as `$\det(A - \lambda I)$` to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Eigenvalues

| Domain | Application | Role of Eigenvalue |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Mechanical / Civil** | Modal analysis of bridges, skyscrapers | Natural frequencies = eigenvalues of mass-stiffness matrix $K M^{-1}$ |
| **Data Science / ML** | Principal Component Analysis (PCA) | Largest eigenvalues of covariance matrix $\Sigma$ = directions of max variance |
| **Computer Science** | Google's PageRank | Dominant eigenvector of the link matrix gives webpage importance |
| **Electrical** | Circuit resonance, filter design | Poles of transfer function = eigenvalues of state-space matrix |
| **Graph Theory** | Spectral clustering, community detection | Fiedler vector = 2nd smallest eigenvalue of graph Laplacian |
| **Differential Equations** | System $\dot{x} = Ax$ solution stability | $\text{Re}(\lambda) < 0 \iff$ asymptotically stable |

### 2.5 Special Properties of Symmetric Matrices (Vital for Data Science)

In Data Science, the **covariance matrix** $\Sigma$ is always real symmetric, so the following powerful guarantees apply:

- All $n$ eigenvalues are **real** ($\lambda_i \in \mathbb{R}$).
- Eigenvectors corresponding to **distinct** eigenvalues are **mutually orthogonal** ($v_i^T v_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$).
- The matrix is always **orthogonally diagonalizable**: $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$ where $Q^T Q = I$.
- All eigenvalues are non-negative if $A$ is positive semi-definite (which $\Sigma$ is, by construction).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From the Eigenvalue Equation to the Characteristic Polynomial

**Starting Premise:** We seek scalars $\lambda$ and non-zero vectors $v$ such that $A v = \lambda v$.

**Step 1** ŌĆö Move all terms to one side. Subtract $\lambda v$ from both sides:

$$A v - \lambda v = 0$$

**Step 2** ŌĆö Factor out the common vector $v$. Note that $\lambda v = (\lambda I) v$ where $I$ is the $n \times n$ identity matrix:

$$A v - (\lambda I) v = 0$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö Use the distributive property of matrix multiplication:

$$(A - \lambda I) v = 0$$

**Step 4** ŌĆö Apply the **Invertible Matrix Theorem**. The homogeneous system $M v = 0$ has a non-trivial solution **if and only if** $M$ is non-invertible, i.e., singular, i.e., has zero determinant:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

**Step 5** ŌĆö Expand the determinant. The result is a polynomial in $\lambda$ of degree exactly $n$ (the leading term is $(-1)^n \lambda^n$):

$$p(\lambda) = (-1)^n \lambda^n + c_{n-1} \lambda^{n-1} + \cdots + c_1 \lambda + c_0 = 0$$

**Step 6** ŌĆö By the **Fundamental Theorem of Algebra**, a degree-$n$ polynomial has exactly $n$ roots in $\mathbb{C}$ (counted with multiplicity). These are the eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$.

### 3.2 Proof of the Trace and Determinant Theorems

**Claim:** For any $n \times n$ matrix $A$ with eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \text{tr}(A) \quad \text{and} \quad \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \det(A)$$

**Proof Sketch:**

The characteristic polynomial factors as:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (-1)^n (\lambda - \lambda_1)(\lambda - \lambda_2) \cdots (\lambda - \lambda_n)$$

Expanding the right-hand side and comparing coefficients with the standard expansion $\det(A - \lambda I) = (-1)^n \lambda^n + (-1)^{n-1} \text{tr}(A) \lambda^{n-1} + \cdots + \det(A)$:

- **Coefficient of $\lambda^{n-1}$**: $(-1)^{n-1} \sum \lambda_i = (-1)^{n-1} \text{tr}(A) \Rightarrow \sum \lambda_i = \text{tr}(A)$.
- **Constant term** (set $\lambda = 0$): $\det(A) = (-1)^n \prod(-\lambda_i) = \prod \lambda_i$.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example ŌĆö KTU Board Style (14-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** Find the eigenvalues and corresponding eigenvectors of the matrix:

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$$

Verify the result using the trace and determinant theorems.

---

#### Step 1: Form the characteristic matrix $A - \lambda I$

Subtract $\lambda$ from each diagonal entry of $A$:

$$A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} 4 - \lambda & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - \lambda \end{pmatrix}$$

#### Step 2: Set up the characteristic equation

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \begin{vmatrix} 4 - \lambda & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0$$

#### Step 3: Compute the determinant using the $2 \times 2$ formula $\det \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} = ad - bc$

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (4 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - (1)(2) = 0$$

#### Step 4: Expand the product

$$(4 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) = 12 - 4\lambda - 3\lambda + \lambda^2 = \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 12$$

So the characteristic equation becomes:

$$\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 12 - 2 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 = 0$$

#### Step 5: Solve the quadratic equation

Factor the quadratic by finding two numbers that multiply to $10$ and add to $-7$: these are $-5$ and $-2$:

$$(\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 2) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = 5, \quad \lambda_2 = 2}$$

#### Step 6: Verification using the Trace and Determinant Theorems

- $\text{tr}(A) = 4 + 3 = 7$, and $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 5 + 2 = 7$ Ō£ō
- $\det(A) = (4)(3) - (1)(2) = 12 - 2 = 10$, and $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 = 5 \times 2 = 10$ Ō£ō

#### Step 7: Find the eigenvector $v_1$ for $\lambda_1 = 5$

Substitute $\lambda = 5$ into $(A - \lambda I) v = 0$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 4 - 5 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - 5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

This gives the single equation $-x + y = 0$, i.e., $y = x$. The eigenvector is:

$$v_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \quad (\text{after normalization})$$

#### Step 8: Find the eigenvector $v_2$ for $\lambda_2 = 2$

Substitute $\lambda = 2$ into $(A - \lambda I) v = 0$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 4 - 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

This gives $2x + y = 0$, i.e., $y = -2x$. The eigenvector is:

$$v_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix} \quad (\text{after normalization})$$

#### Step 9: Final Verification

Check $A v_1 = 5 v_1$:

$$A v_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} = 5 \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = 5 v_1 \quad \checkmark$$

Check $A v_2 = 2 v_2$:

$$A v_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix} = 2 \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix} = 2 v_2 \quad \checkmark$$

#### Step 10: Orthogonality Check (since $A$ is NOT symmetric, orthogonality is NOT guaranteed)

$$v_1^T v_2 = (1)(1) + (1)(-2) = 1 - 2 = -1 \neq 0$$

This confirms that non-symmetric matrices have eigenvectors that are generally **not orthogonal** ŌĆö a critical distinction for the KTU exam.

### 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö $3 \times 3$ Symmetric Matrix (PCA Flavor)

**Problem:** Find the eigenvalues of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, which is the discrete Laplacian (used in spectral graph theory).

**Step 1**: Set up $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$:

$$\det \begin{pmatrix} 2 - \lambda & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 - \lambda & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = 0$$

**Step 2**: Cofactor expansion along the first row:

$$(2 - \lambda) \begin{vmatrix} 2 - \lambda & -1 \\ -1 & 2 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} - (-1) \begin{vmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ 0 & 2 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} + 0 = 0$$

**Step 3**: Evaluate the $2 \times 2$ determinants:

$$\begin{vmatrix} 2 - \lambda & -1 \\ -1 & 2 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = (2 - \lambda)^2 - 1 = \lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 3$$

$$\begin{vmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ 0 & 2 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = -1(2 - \lambda) - 0 = \lambda - 2$$

**Step 4**: Substitute and simplify:

$$(2 - \lambda)(\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 3) + (\lambda - 2) = 0$$

Note $(\lambda - 2) = -(2 - \lambda)$, so factor out $(2 - \lambda)$:

$$(2 - \lambda) \left[ \lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 3 - 1 \right] = 0$$

$$(2 - \lambda)(\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 2) = 0$$

**Step 5**: Solve each factor. The first gives $\lambda_1 = 2$. The second requires the quadratic formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 8}}{2} = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{8}}{2} = 2 \pm \sqrt{2}$$

**Final Eigenvalues:**

$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586, \quad \lambda_2 = 2, \quad \lambda_3 = 2 + \sqrt{2} \approx 3.414}$$

**Verification:** $\sum \lambda_i = (2 - \sqrt{2}) + 2 + (2 + \sqrt{2}) = 6 = \text{tr}(A)$ Ō£ō

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Eigenvalue Computation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the complete functional flow of an eigenvalue computation engine, from raw matrix input to validated eigenpairs. This is the architecture used internally by numerical libraries such as LAPACK, NumPy's `numpy.linalg.eig`, and MATLAB's `eig()`.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Matrix A] --> B[Validate Dimensions]
    B --> C{Is A square?}
    C -- No --> D[Error: Eigenvalues undefined]
    C -- Yes --> E[Form A minus lambda I]
    E --> F[Compute Characteristic Determinant]
    F --> G[Build Polynomial p of lambda]
    G --> H{Choose Solver}
    H --> I1[Closed-form for n less than 3]
    H --> I2[Rational Root Theorem for n equals 3]
    H --> I3[QR Algorithm for n greater than 3]
    I1 --> J[Root Set lambda 1, lambda 2, ...]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[For each lambda i, solve AV equals lambda i V]
    K --> L[Row reduce A minus lambda i I]
    L --> M[Extract Null Space Basis]
    M --> N[Normalize to unit length]
    N --> O[Output Eigenpairs]
    O --> P[Validation Layer]
    P --> Q{Trace check: sum lambda i equals tr A?}
    P --> R{Determinant check: prod lambda i equals det A?}
    Q --> S[Eigenpairs Confirmed]
    R --> S
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Diagram: The Action of A on its Eigenvectors

This diagram visualizes the geometric "stretching-only" property of eigenvectors ŌĆö the most intuitive way to internalize the concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputSpace ["Original Vector Space"]
        V1[Arbitrary vector v arbitrary]
        V2[Eigenvector v1 direction]
        V3[Eigenvector v2 direction]
    end

    subgraph OutputSpace ["After Transformation A"]
        W1[Rotated and stretched v arbitrary]
        W2[Only stretched v1 by factor lambda 1]
        W3[Only stretched v2 by factor lambda 2]
    end

    V1 -- A applied --> W1
    V2 -- A applied --> W2
    V3 -- A applied --> W3

    style V1 fill:#fdd
    style W1 fill:#fdd
    style V2 fill:#dfd
    style W2 fill:#dfd
    style V3 fill:#ddf
    style W3 fill:#ddf
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *red* (arbitrary) vector gets rotated and stretched ŌĆö its direction changes. The *green* and *blue* eigenvectors lie along the **principal axes** of the transformation: they are only stretched (or shrunk), never rotated. This is precisely why eigenvectors are the natural coordinate system for analyzing any linear map.

### 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Use Eigenvalues in Data Science

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Data Science Problem] --> Q1{Is the data matrix M x N with M much greater than N?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Compute covariance matrix Sigma of size N x N]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is the data on a graph or network?}
    Q2 --> PCA[Use eigendecomposition of Sigma for PCA]
    PCA --> Out1[Top k eigenvectors give k principal components]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4[Form graph Laplacian L equals D minus W]
    Q4 --> Spec[Use Fiedler eigenvector for spectral clustering]
    Spec --> Out2[2nd smallest eigenvector of L]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5{Is there a system of linear ODEs?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> ODE[Diagonalize coefficient matrix A]
    ODE --> Out3[Solution is sum of e to the lambda i t times v i]
    Q5 -- No --> Stable[No direct eigenvalue application]
```
