- Adding a useless feature ŌåÆ $SS_{res}$ barely drops, but the penalty factor $\frac{N-1}{N-p-1}$ grows ŌåÆ $\bar{R}^2$ **decreases** (useless feature is punished).

Hence, $\bar{R}^2$ acts as a *self-correcting* metric, ensuring fair comparison across models of different complexity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute residuals $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$.**

| Point | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $e_i$ | $e_i^2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 100 | 110 | -10 | 100 |
| 2 | 200 | 190 | 10 | 100 |
| 3 | 300 | 310 | -10 | 100 |
| 4 | 400 | 380 | 20 | 400 |
| 5 | 500 | 520 | -20 | 400 |
| **Sum** | | | **-10** | **1100** |

**Step 2 ŌĆö Mean of $y$.**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{100+200+300+400+500}{5} = \frac{1500}{5} = 300$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö MAE.**

$$MAE = \frac{\sum \vert e_i \vert}{N} = \frac{10+10+10+20+20}{5} = \frac{70}{5} = 14$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö MSE.**

$$MSE = \frac{\sum e_i^2}{N} = \frac{1100}{5} = 220$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö RMSE.**

$$RMSE = \sqrt{220} \approx 14.83$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Total Sum of Squares $SS_{tot}$.**

$$SS_{tot} = (100-300)^2 + (200-300)^2 + (300-300)^2 + (400-300)^2 + (500-300)^2$$
$$= 40000 + 10000 + 0 + 10000 + 40000 = 100000$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö R┬▓ Score.**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{1100}{100000} = 1 - 0.011 = 0.989$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Interpretation.**
- **$R^2 = 0.989$** means the model explains **98.9%** of the variance ŌĆö an excellent fit.
- **MAE = 14** ŌåÆ on average, predictions deviate by 14 units.
- **RMSE = 14.83 > MAE = 14** ŌåÆ the larger errors ($-20, +20$) are being amplified by squaring, as expected.
- Both MAE and RMSE are in the **same unit as $y$**, making them directly interpretable in business terms. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall:** Many students swap the formulas. *Memory aid:* Precision ŌåÆ "of the *Predicted* Positives, how correct?" Recall ŌåÆ "of the *Real* Positives, how retrieved?"
> 2. **Forgetting the unit of MSE vs. RMSE:** MSE is in *squared* units; RMSE is in the *original* unit. Examiners deduct marks if you claim "MAE and MSE have the same unit."
> 3. **Reporting R┬▓ without context:** R┬▓ alone is not enough. Always mention that it can be **negative** for poor models, and use **Adjusted R┬▓** when comparing models with different feature counts.
> 4. **Confusing F1 with arithmetic mean:** F1 is the **harmonic** mean, not arithmetic. Writing $F_1 = \frac{P+R}{2}$ will cost you 2 marks.
> 5. **Using Accuracy for imbalanced data:** If the question says "the dataset has 95% negatives," a sloppy "we use accuracy" answer is *guaranteed* to lose marks. Use F1 or Recall.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for End-Semester Preparation:**

- Ō£ģ **Confusion Matrix** is the *gateway* to all classification metrics. Master the four cells: **TP, TN, FP, FN**.
- Ō£ģ **Accuracy** = $\frac{TP+TN}{N}$. Use ONLY for balanced datasets. Beware the **Accuracy Paradox** on imbalanced data.
- Ō£ģ **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ ŌĆö punish false alarms. **Recall** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ ŌĆö punish missed detections.
- Ō£ģ **F1-Score** = Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. Use it when both errors are equally costly. Use $F_\beta$ to bias toward Precision ($\beta<1$) or Recall ($\beta>1$).
- Ō£ģ **ROC-AUC** is threshold-independent. AUC = 0.5 means random; AUC = 1.0 means perfect.
- Ō£ģ **Log Loss** captures probabilistic confidence. Punishes confident wrong predictions heavily.
- Ō£ģ **MAE** = L1 norm, robust to outliers, same unit as $y$. **MSE** = L2 norm, sensitive to outliers, squared unit. **RMSE** = $\sqrt{MSE}$, same unit as $y$.
- Ō£ģ **R┬▓** Ōłł $(-\infty, 1]$. $R^2 = 1$ perfect; $R^2 = 0$ no better than mean; **R┬▓ can be negative** for very bad models.
- Ō£ģ **Adjusted R┬▓** penalizes irrelevant features. Use it whenever you compare models with different feature counts.
- Ō£ģ **Silhouette Score** Ōłł $[-1, 1]$. Higher = better-defined clusters. **Davies-Bouldin** ŌåÆ lower is better. **Calinski-Harabasz** ŌåÆ higher is better.
- Ō£ģ **Bias-Variance Decomposition**: Total Error = Bias┬▓ + Variance + Irreducible Noise. This is the *theoretical foundation* of why we cannot have a model that is simultaneously low-bias AND low-variance.
- Ō£ģ **Metric selection is problem-specific.** Always justify *why* you chose a metric in exam answers ŌĆö KTU examiners reward this reasoning (2ŌĆō3 marks per question).
- Ō£ģ **For exam writing:** Always show the *numerator* and *denominator* explicitly when computing metrics. Do not skip algebraic transitions.

---

*End of Module 3 ŌĆö Performance Measures ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Comprehensive Notes*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logistic Regression Implementation in R
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT722 - DATA SCIENCE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Logistic Regression Implementation in R

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Logistic Regression** is a supervised machine learning classification algorithm used to model the probability of a categorical (typically binary) dependent variable as a function of one or more independent predictor variables. Despite its name, it is a *classification* technique, not a regression technique in the continuous-output sense.

Mathematically, it estimates the parameters of a logistic (sigmoidal) function to map any real-valued input into a probability value bounded in the interval $(0, 1)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Logistic regression is foundational to generalized linear models (GLM). In R, it is implemented using the `glm()` function with `family = binomial(link = "logit")`. The output is a probability, which is then thresholded (typically at **0.5**) to assign class labels.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor trying to predict whether a patient has a particular disease based on symptoms like age, blood pressure, and cholesterol level. The doctor does not want a "raw score" ŌĆö she wants a *probability* between 0 and 1.

**Logistic Regression works exactly like this clinical reasoning process:**

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Linear Combination:** Combine the input features (age, BP, cholesterol) with weights, just like linear regression.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Squash the Output:** Pass this linear combination through a special "S-curve" function called the **sigmoid** that squashes any real number into a probability between 0 and 1.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Decision Rule:** If the probability is > 0.5, classify the patient as "diseased"; otherwise, "healthy."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not Linear Regression for classification?** Linear regression can output values like -2 or 7, which are meaningless as probabilities. The sigmoid function solves this by mapping everything neatly into $(0, 1)$.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Sigmoid Function** | The activation/logistic function that maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0, 1)$ |
| **Logit** | The natural log of the odds, $\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$ |
| **Odds** | The ratio $\frac{p}{1-p}$ where $p$ is probability of success |
| **MLE** | Maximum Likelihood Estimation ŌĆö the method to fit model parameters |
| **Threshold** | Cut-off value (default **0.5**) for converting probability to class label |
| **Confusion Matrix** | $2 \times 2$ table comparing predicted vs. actual class labels |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Breakdown

**How Logistic Regression Works (The "Why" and "How"):**

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Model the log-odds as linear:** Unlike linear regression that models $y$ directly, logistic regression models the *log-odds* of the outcome as a linear combination of features. This is the "Why": log-odds are unbounded in $\mathbb{R}$, so a linear function makes sense.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the inverse logit (sigmoid):** To recover the probability $p$ from the log-odds, apply the inverse function ŌĆö the sigmoid. This is the "How" we convert a real number back to a probability.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Estimate parameters using MLE:** There is no closed-form solution for logistic regression (unlike linear regression's normal equations). We use **Maximum Likelihood Estimation** or equivalently minimize the **log-loss** cost function via iterative optimization (e.g., iteratively reweighted least squares, gradient descent, or L-BFGS in R).

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Classify using thresholding:** Once we have $p(x)$, apply a threshold $\tau$ (default 0.5) to assign the final class label.

### 2.2 The Sigmoid Function ŌĆö Heart of the Algorithm

The sigmoid (logistic) function is defined as:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

Its key properties:
- $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ for all $z \in \mathbb{R}$
- $\sigma(0) = 0.5$
- $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ ŌĆö this elegant derivative makes gradient computation efficient.

### 2.3 The Log-Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy) Cost Function

For $m$ training samples, the cost function (negative log-likelihood) is:

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{p}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \right]$$

where $\hat{p}^{(i)} = \sigma(z^{(i)})$ and $z^{(i)} = \theta^T x^{(i)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Log-Loss and not MSE?** Using Mean Squared Error with sigmoid leads to a *non-convex* cost surface with many local minima. Log-loss yields a **convex** cost function, guaranteeing convergence to the global minimum.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Sigmoid function | Output: dimensionless probability |
| $z = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \ldots + \theta_n x_n$ | Linear predictor | $z \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $\hat{p} = \sigma(z)$ | Predicted probability | $\hat{p} \in (0,1)$ |
| $\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$ | Log-odds | Unbounded |
| $J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum [y\log\hat{p} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{p})]$ | Log-loss / BCE | Unitless, $\geq 0$ |
| $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Classification accuracy | Range: $[0,1]$ |
| $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Positive predictive value | Range: $[0,1]$ |
| $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Sensitivity / TPR | Range: $[0,1]$ |
| $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean | Range: $[0,1]$ |
| $\text{AUC}$ | Area Under ROC Curve | Range: $[0,1]$, **0.5 = random** |
| $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ | Gradient descent update | $\alpha$ = learning rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Logistic regression is widely used in medical diagnosis (disease/no disease), spam filtering (spam/ham), credit scoring (default/no-default), and A/B testing (conversion/no-conversion) ŌĆö all critical classification problems in modern industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Log-Odds to Sigmoid

We start by modeling the **log-odds** of the probability $p$ as a linear function of the features:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \theta^T x = z$$

**Step A ŌĆö Exponentiate both sides** to remove the natural logarithm:

$$\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{z}$$

**Step B ŌĆö Multiply both sides by $(1-p)$:**

$$p = (1-p) \cdot e^{z}$$

**Step C ŌĆö Expand the right side:**

$$p = e^{z} - p \cdot e^{z}$$

**Step D ŌĆö Collect $p$ terms on the left side:**

$$p + p \cdot e^{z} = e^{z}$$

**Step E ŌĆö Factor out $p$:**

$$p(1 + e^{z}) = e^{z}$$

**Step F ŌĆö Solve for $p$ (this is the sigmoid function):**

$$p = \frac{e^{z}}{1 + e^{z}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = \sigma(z)$$

This is a clean derivation showing that the sigmoid is simply the **inverse** of the logit function.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient of the Log-Loss

We need the derivative of the cost $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta_j$ for gradient descent. We use the chain rule on a single sample first:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{p}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{p}}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial \theta_j}$$

**Step A ŌĆö Compute $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{p}}$** from the log-loss for a single sample ($J = -[y\log\hat{p} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{p})]$):

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{p}} = -\frac{y}{\hat{p}} + \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{p}}$$

**Step B ŌĆö Compute $\frac{\partial \hat{p}}{\partial z}$** using the sigmoid derivative identity $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) = \hat{p}(1-\hat{p})$:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{p}}{\partial z} = \hat{p}(1-\hat{p})$$

**Step C ŌĆö Compute $\frac{\partial z}{\partial \theta_j}$** from $z = \theta^T x$:

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial \theta_j} = x_j$$

**Step D ŌĆö Multiply all three components:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \left(-\frac{y}{\hat{p}} + \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{p}}\right) \cdot \hat{p}(1-\hat{p}) \cdot x_j$$

**Step E ŌĆö Simplify the bracket** by finding a common denominator:

$$-\frac{y}{\hat{p}} + \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{p}} = \frac{-y(1-\hat{p}) + (1-y)\hat{p}}{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})} = \frac{\hat{p} - y}{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}$$

**Step F ŌĆö Cancel $\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})$** with the same term in the chain:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = (\hat{p} - y) \cdot x_j$$

**Final compact form for $m$ samples:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{p}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

This beautiful result is what R's `glm()` optimizer (typically L-BFGS) computes internally.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Manual Logistic Regression on 5 Samples

**Given training data** (single feature $x$, binary label $y$):

| Sample $i$ | $x^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | 0 |
| 2 | 1.0 | 0 |
| 3 | 2.0 | 1 |
| 4 | 2.5 | 1 |
| 5 | 3.0 | 1 |

**Assume the model has parameters:** $\theta_0 = -2.0$, $\theta_1 = 1.0$. Compute predicted probabilities:

**For $i=1$:** $z = -2.0 + 1.0 \times 0.5 = -1.5$

$$\hat{p}_1 = \frac{1}{1+e^{1.5}} = \frac{1}{1+4.4817} \approx 0.1824$$

**For $i=2$:** $z = -2.0 + 1.0 \times 1.0 = -1.0$

$$\hat{p}_2 = \frac{1}{1+e^{1.0}} = \frac{1}{1+2.7183} \approx 0.2689$$

**For $i=3$:** $z = -2.0 + 1.0 \times 2.0 = 0.0$

$$\hat{p}_3 = \frac{1}{1+e^{0}} = 0.5000$$

**For $i=4$:** $z = -2.0 + 1.0 \times 2.5 = 0.5$

$$\hat{p}_4 = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.5}} = \frac{1}{1+0.6065} \approx 0.6225$$

**For $i=5$:** $z = -2.0 + 1.0 \times 3.0 = 1.0$

$$\hat{p}_5 = \frac{1}{1+e^{-1.0}} = \frac{1}{1+0.3679} \approx 0.7311$$

**Compute the Log-Loss:**

$$J = -\frac{1}{5}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{5} y^{(i)}\log\hat{p}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-\hat{p}^{(i)})\right]$$

Breaking it down sample-by-sample:

- $i=1$ ($y=0$): contribution $= -(1-0)\log(1-0.1824) = -\log(0.8176) \approx 0.2014$
- $i=2$ ($y=0$): contribution $= -\log(1-0.2689) = -\log(0.7311) \approx 0.3133$
- $i=3$ ($y=1$): contribution $= -\log(0.5000) \approx 0.6931$
- $i=4$ ($y=1$): contribution $= -\log(0.6225) \approx 0.4741$
- $i=5$ ($y=1$): contribution $= -\log(0.7311) \approx 0.3133$

**Sum** $= 0.2014 + 0.3133 + 0.6931 + 0.4741 + 0.3133 = 1.9952$

$$J = \frac{1.9952}{5} \approx 0.3990$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the loss for the *current* parameters. The optimizer in R will iteratively update $\theta$ to **minimize** this value ŌĆö that is the essence of MLE.

### 3.4 R Implementation ŌĆö End-to-End Working Code

```r
# ============================================================
# LOGISTIC REGRESSION IMPLEMENTATION IN R
# Dataset: mtcars (built-in) ŌĆö Predict transmission type (am)
# ============================================================

# Step 1: Load and prepare data
data(mtcars)
mtcars$am <- factor(mtcars$am, levels = c(0, 1),
                     labels = c("Automatic", "Manual"))

# Step 2: Exploratory split (70/30)
set.seed(42)
train_idx <- sample(1:nrow(mtcars), size = 0.7 * nrow(mtcars))
train_data <- mtcars[train_idx, ]
test_data  <- mtcars[-train_idx, ]

# Step 3: Fit logistic regression model using glm()
model <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt + hp,
             data = train_data,
             family = binomial(link = "logit"))

# Step 4: View the model summary
print(summary(model))

# Step 5: Predict probabilities on test set
prob_test <- predict(model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")

# Step 6: Convert probabilities to class labels (threshold = 0.5)
class_pred <- ifelse(prob_test >= 0.5, "Manual", "Automatic")
class_pred <- factor(class_pred, levels = c("Automatic", "Manual"))

# Step 7: Build confusion matrix
actual <- test_data$am
conf_mat <- table(Predicted = class_pred, Actual = actual)
print(conf_mat)

# Step 8: Compute evaluation metrics
TP <- conf_mat["Manual", "Manual"]
TN <- conf_mat["Automatic", "Automatic"]
FP <- conf_mat["Manual", "Automatic"]
FN <- conf_mat["Automatic", "Manual"]

accuracy  <- (TP + TN) / sum(conf_mat)
precision <- TP / (TP + FP)
recall    <- TP / (TP + FN)
f1_score  <- 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)

cat("Accuracy :", round(accuracy, 4), "\n")
cat("Precision:", round(precision, 4), "\n")
cat("Recall   :", round(recall, 4), "\n")
cat("F1-Score :", round(f1_score, 4), "\n")

# Step 9: ROC curve and AUC (using pROC package)
# install.packages("pROC")
library(pROC)
roc_obj <- roc(actual, prob_test, levels = c("Automatic", "Manual"))
plot(roc_obj, col = "blue", main = "ROC Curve")
cat("AUC:", round(auc(roc_obj), 4), "\n")
```

**Key R Functions Used:**

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `glm()` | Generalized Linear Model ŌĆö fits logistic regression |
| `family = binomial(link = "logit")` | Specifies logistic regression |
| `predict(..., type = "response")` | Returns probabilities (not logits) |
| `ifelse(..., 0.5, ...)` | Applies the 0.5 threshold |
| `table()` | Builds the confusion matrix |
| `roc()` / `auc()` | Plots ROC and computes AUC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Logistic Regression Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load Dataset] --> B[Exploratory Data Analysis]
    B --> C{Handle Missing Values}
    C --> D[Encode Categorical Variables]
    D --> E[Train-Test Split 70:30]
    E --> F[Fit glm family binomial link logit]
    F --> G[Examine Summary Coefficients]
    G --> H[Predict type response probabilities]
    H --> I[Apply Threshold 0.5]
    I --> J[Build Confusion Matrix]
    J --> K[Compute Accuracy Precision Recall F1]
    K --> L[Plot ROC and Compute AUC]
    L --> M{AUC greater than 0.8}
    M -->|Yes| N[Accept Model]
    M -->|No| O[Feature Engineering or Regularization]
    O --> F
    N --> P[End]
```

### 4.2 Mathematical Computation Flow (Sigmoid Computation Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Features x1 x2 xn] --> B[Linear Combination z = theta0 + sum thetaj xj]
    B --> C[Apply Sigmoid Function]
    C --> D[Probability p in 0 1]
    D --> E{Threshold 0.5}
    E -->|p greater than 0.5| F[Class 1 Positive]
    E -->|p less than 0.5| G[Class 0 Negative]
```

### 4.3 Confusion Matrix Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Confusion Matrix 2x2] --> B[Top-Left: True Negative TN]
    A --> C[Top-Right: False Positive FP Type I Error]
    A --> D[Bottom-Left: False Negative FN Type II Error]
    A --> E[Bottom-Right: True Positive TP]
    B --> F[Accuracy = TP+TN divided by Total]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    E --> G[Recall TPR = TP divided by TP+FN]
    C --> H[Precision = TP divided by TP+FP]
    G --> I[F1 Score Harmonic Mean]
    H --> I
```

### 4.4 MLE Optimization Loop (How R Fits the Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize theta randomly or zeros] --> B[Compute predicted probabilities p]
    B --> C[Compute Log-Loss J theta]
    C --> D[Compute Gradient of J wrt theta]
    D --> E[Update theta using L-BFGS or IRLS]
    E --> F{Converged}
    F -->|No| B
    F -->|Yes| G[Return Optimized theta]
    G --> H[Output Model Coefficients]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**
*Define the sigmoid function. Why is it preferred over a linear function for binary classification?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **sigmoid function** is defined as $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, which maps any real-valued input $z \in \mathbb{R}$ to an output in the open interval $(0, 1)$.

**Why preferred for binary classification:**

- **Bounded output:** Its range is strictly $(0, 1)$, making the output directly interpretable as a probability. A linear function $z = \theta^T x$ can output any real number, which is meaningless as a probability.
- **Smooth gradient:** The derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ is simple and computationally efficient for gradient-based optimization.
- **Probabilistic interpretation:** Outputs can be thresholded (default 0.5) to obtain class labels naturally.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often state the formula but forget to mention the **range $(0,1)$** and the *probabilistic interpretation*. Mentioning both secures full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the logit transformation and its role in logistic regression.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **logit transformation** is defined as the natural logarithm of the odds:

$$\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$$

**Role in logistic regression:**

- It transforms a probability $p \in (0,1)$ into an unbounded real number $\text{logit}(p) \in (mathbb R)$.
- This is the *key trick*: instead of modeling the bounded probability $p$ as a linear function of features (which would fail), logistic regression models the **log-odds** as a linear function: $\text{logit}(p) = \theta^T x$.
- The sigmoid function is the **inverse** of the logit, used to recover $p$ from $z = \theta^T x$.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

#### Ō£” QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the logistic regression algorithm. Derive the gradient update rule for the log-loss cost function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement logistic regression in R for the `iris` dataset to classify `setosa` vs. `versicolor` using `Petal.Length` and `Petal.Width` as predictors. Show the model summary, confusion matrix, accuracy, and AUC. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The working of logistic regression is best understood in three stages ŌĆö Linear Combination ŌåÆ Sigmoid Squash ŌåÆ Threshold Decision:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Linear Combination:** Compute $z = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \ldots + \theta_n x_n$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sigmoid:** $\hat{p} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Threshold:** Class label $= 1$ if $\hat{p} \geq 0.5$, else $0$.

**Derivation of Gradient Update Rule:**

The cost function (log-loss) for a single training example is:

$$J(\theta) = -\left[y \log(\hat{p}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{p})\right]$$

We showed in Section 3.2 that:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = (\hat{p} - y) \, x_j$$

The **gradient update rule** with learning rate $\alpha$ is therefore:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot (\hat{p}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \, x_j^{(i)}$$

This update is applied iteratively (in batches) until the cost function converges to its minimum. R's `glm()` uses L-BFGS or IRLS ŌĆö both of which internally use the same gradient.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

```r
# Prepare binary classification data
data(iris)
iris_bin <- subset(iris, Species %in% c("setosa", "versicolor"))
iris_bin$Species <- factor(iris_bin$Species)

# Train/test split
set.seed(123)
idx <- sample(1:nrow(iris_bin), 0.7 * nrow(iris_bin))
train <- iris_bin[idx, ]
test  <- iris_bin[-idx, ]

# Fit logistic regression
model <- glm(Species ~ Petal.Length + Petal.Width,
             data = train, family = binomial)

# Predictions
prob <- predict(model, newdata = test, type = "response")
pred <- ifelse(prob >= 0.5, "versicolor", "setosa")
pred <- factor(pred, levels = levels(test$Species))

# Confusion matrix and accuracy
cm <- table(Predicted = pred, Actual = test$Species)
print(cm)
accuracy <- sum(diag(cm)) / sum(cm)
print(accuracy)

# AUC
library(pROC)
roc_obj <- roc(test$Species, prob, levels = c("setosa", "versicolor"))
print(auc(roc_obj))
```

**Expected Output (illustrative):**
- Confusion matrix showing mostly correct classifications
- Accuracy typically > **0.95** for this trivial dataset
- AUC typically > **0.99** (near-perfect separation since setosa/versicolor are linearly separable by petal dimensions)

---

#### Ō£” QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**(a)** Differentiate between linear regression and logistic regression. Explain why log-loss (binary cross-entropy) is used instead of mean squared error in logistic regression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bank wants to predict loan default (Yes/No) based on `income` and `debt_ratio`. Write complete R code to: (i) create a sample data frame with 20 observations, (ii) fit a logistic regression model, (iii) display model summary with odds ratios, (iv) evaluate the model on a test set using accuracy and a confusion matrix. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Linear Regression | Logistic Regression |
|---|---|---|
| **Output type** | Continuous real number | Probability $\in (0,1)$, then class |
| **Function** | Identity $y = \theta^T x$ | Sigmoid $\hat{p} = \sigma(\theta^T x)$ |
| **Cost function** | Mean Squared Error | Log-Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy) |
| **Optimization** | Closed-form (Normal Eq.) | Iterative (MLE, L-BFGS, IRLS) |
| **Use case** | Regression tasks | Classification tasks |
| **Assumptions** | Linearity, normality of residuals | Linearity in log-odds, no normality |

**Why Log-Loss instead of MSE?**

Using MSE with the sigmoid function produces a **non-convex** cost surface:

$$J_{MSE} = \frac{1}{m}\sum (\sigma(\theta^T x) - y)^2$$

Because $\sigma$ is non-linear, the composition $\sigma(\theta^T x)$ makes $J_{MSE}$ have many local minima ŌĆö gradient descent may get stuck.

In contrast, the **log-loss** function:

$$J_{LL} = -\frac{1}{m}\sum\left[y\log\hat{p} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{p})\right]$$

is **convex** when the model is well-specified, guaranteeing a single global minimum. Additionally, log-loss is the direct negative of the log-likelihood, so minimizing it is equivalent to MLE ŌĆö a statistically principled objective.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

```r
# (i) Create sample data frame
set.seed(2024)
income     <- round(rnorm(20, mean = 50000, sd = 15000), 2)
debt_ratio <- round(runif(20, min = 0.1, max = 0.8), 2)
default    <- factor(ifelse(income * 0.000005 + debt_ratio * 0.5 +
                            rnorm(20, 0, 0.1) > 0.4, "Yes", "No"))
bank_data  <- data.frame(income, debt_ratio, default)

# Train/test split
train_idx <- sample(1:20, 14)
train <- bank_data[train_idx, ]
test  <- bank_data[-train_idx, ]

# (ii) Fit logistic regression
model <- glm(default ~ income + debt_ratio,
             data = train, family = binomial)

# (iii) Model summary and odds ratios
summary(model)
odds_ratios <- exp(coef(model))
print(odds_ratios)

# (iv) Evaluation on test set
prob <- predict(model, newdata = test, type = "response")
pred <- factor(ifelse(prob >= 0.5, "Yes", "No"),
               levels = c("No", "Yes"))
cm   <- table(Predicted = pred, Actual = test$default)
print(cm)
accuracy <- sum(diag(cm)) / sum(cm)
cat("Test Accuracy:", round(accuracy, 4), "\n")
```

**Explanation of Odds Ratios:**
- `exp(coef(model))` gives the **odds ratio** for each predictor.
- If `odds_ratio > 1`, a unit increase in that predictor *increases* the odds of default.
- If `odds_ratio < 1`, a unit increase *decreases* the odds of default.
- E.g., if `debt_ratio` odds ratio = 3.2, then for each 1-unit increase in debt ratio, the odds of default multiply by 3.2.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `type = "response"`** in `predict()` ŌĆö without it, R returns logits (z-values), not probabilities. Thresholding logits will give **completely wrong** predictions and zero marks.
> 2. **Confusing accuracy with precision/recall** ŌĆö accuracy alone is misleading for imbalanced datasets; examiners expect students to mention this.
> 3. **Not setting the seed (`set.seed()`)** ŌĆö makes the answer non-reproducible; partial mark deduction.
> 4. **Misinterpreting coefficients as probabilities** ŌĆö coefficients $\theta_j$ are in log-odds space; one must exponentiate to get odds ratios.

---

### ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Logistic Regression is a CLASSIFICATION algorithm**, not regression in the continuous sense.
- The **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0,1)$.
- The **logit** is the inverse of the sigmoid: $\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$.
- Logistic regression models **log-odds linearly**: $\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \theta^T x$.
- The cost function is **log-loss (binary cross-entropy)** ŌĆö never MSE ŌĆö because log-loss is convex.
- **MLE** is used for parameter estimation; no closed-form solution exists.
- In R, the model is fit using `glm(formula, data, family = binomial(link = "logit"))`.
- **Always use `type = "response"`** in `predict()` to get probabilities.
- **Default threshold is 0.5**; can be tuned for precision-recall trade-off.
- **Evaluation metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, ROC-AUC.
- **Odds ratio** = $\exp(\theta_j)$ ŌĆö interpretation: change in odds per unit change in $x_j$.
- **Assumptions:** Linearity in log-odds (not in raw features), independence of observations, no perfect separation.
- **Multicollinearity** can inflate coefficient variance ŌĆö check with VIF if needed.
- For **multiclass classification** ($K > 2$ classes), use multinomial logistic regression or one-vs-rest with `family = binomial` repeatedly.
- **Regularization** variants: `glmnet::glmnet()` for L1 (Lasso) or L2 (Ridge) penalized logistic regression.

> **All the best for your KTU examinations!** ŌĆö *aim for clarity, derivations, and clean R code.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT722 - DATA SCIENCE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN)** is a fundamental, non-parametric, instance-based supervised learning algorithm used for both **classification** and **regression** tasks. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (OEITT722 ŌĆō Data Science for Engineers), kNN is formally defined as a *lazy learning* (or *memory-based learning*) technique in which the classification of a query instance is determined by the **majority class label** among its **k most similar training examples** in the multi-dimensional feature space. Similarity is quantified through a **distance metric**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board Standard):** kNN classifies an unseen data point by assigning it the class that is most frequent among the k training points that lie closest to it in the feature space, where "closeness" is measured using a specified distance function.

### Key Terminology Used by KTU Examiners

- **k**: The number of nearest neighbors considered. **k Ōłł ŌäĢ, k Ōēź 1**.
- **Distance Metric**: A mathematical function $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ quantifying dissimilarity between two feature vectors.
- **Feature Space**: The n-dimensional Euclidean-like space where each axis represents one feature.
- **Voting / Aggregation Rule**: The mechanism (majority, weighted, distance-weighted) used to combine the k neighbors' outputs.
- **Lazy Learner**: No explicit model is built; all computation is deferred until query time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Friends Asking for Advice"

Imagine you have moved to a new city and want to know whether the local **vegetable market** is good. You ask your **3 closest friends** (k = 3) who live nearby. Two of them say "the market is excellent" and one says "it is average." By **majority voting**, you conclude the market is excellent. This is *exactly* how kNN works:

| Real-World Analogy | kNN Component |
|---|---|
| "You" (the new person) | **Query / test point** $\mathbf{x}_q$ |
| Your k closest friends | The **k nearest training points** |
| Physical distance to friends | **Distance metric** $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ |
| Majority opinion of friends | **Class label** assigned to $\mathbf{x}_q$ |
| Changing the number of friends (1, 3, 5ŌĆ”) | Varying the **hyperparameter k** |

## 1.3 Why kNN is a "Lazy" Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike eager learners (e.g., Decision Trees, SVM, Linear Regression) that build a generalized model during the *training* phase, **kNN performs zero learning at training time**. It simply **stores the entire training dataset** as-is. All the real computation ŌĆö the distance calculations, the neighbor search, and the vote ŌĆö happens **only when a new test point arrives**. This is why kNN is classified as a *memory-based* or *instance-based* learner.

### Headline Properties to Memorize

- **Non-parametric**: Makes no assumption about the underlying data distribution.
- **Instance-based**: Stores the full training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
- **Supervised**: Requires labelled data.
- **Versatile**: Handles both classification (voting) and regression (averaging).
- **Computationally expensive at inference** time: Complexity is $O(Nd)$ per query, where $N$ is the dataset size and $d$ is the number of features.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The kNN Algorithm ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

The kNN classification procedure can be decomposed into the following rigorous sequence of operations:

1. **Receive Query Point** $\mathbf{x}_q \in \mathbb{R}^d$.
2. **Choose Hyperparameter k**: An odd integer is generally preferred to avoid tie-breaks.
3. **Select a Distance Metric** $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ ŌĆö most commonly **Euclidean**, **Manhattan**, or **Minkowski**.
4. **Compute Pairwise Distances**: For every training point $\mathbf{x}_i$ in $\mathcal{D}$, calculate $d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)$.
5. **Rank Distances**: Sort the distances in ascending order.
6. **Identify Top-k Neighbors**: Extract the k training points with the smallest distances. Denote this set as $\mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)$.
7. **Aggregate Labels**: Apply the voting rule to obtain the predicted class $\hat{y}_q$.
8. **Return Prediction**: Output $\hat{y}_q$.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 4 (distance computation)** is the mathematical heart of kNN; the entire algorithm pivots on how we measure similarity.
- **Step 5ŌĆō6** reduce the entire dataset to a local neighborhood, embodying the principle of *locality*: nearby points are expected to have similar labels.
- **Step 7 (aggregation)** translates a set of numerical observations into a discrete or continuous output. For **classification** it is majority voting; for **regression** it is the **arithmetic mean** (or weighted mean) of the neighbors' target values.

## 2.2 Aggregation Rules

**Classification (Majority Voting):**

$$
\hat{y}_q \;=\; \arg\max_{c \in \mathcal{C}} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)} \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)
$$

where $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all class labels and $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the **indicator function** that returns 1 if the argument is true and 0 otherwise.

**Regression (Mean Aggregation):**

$$
\hat{y}_q \;=\; \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)} y_i
$$

**Distance-Weighted Voting** (an advanced, frequently-asked variant):

$$
\hat{y}_q \;=\; \arg\max_{c \in \mathcal{C}} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)} w_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(y_i = c), \qquad w_i \;=\; \frac{1}{d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) + \epsilon}
$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small constant (e.g., $10^{-6}$) added to prevent division by zero.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet ŌĆö Distance Metrics

| Metric Name | Mathematical Formula (vectors $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^d$) | Order $p$ | Geometric Intuition | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Euclidean** (L2) | $d(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | $p = 2$ | Straight-line "as the crow flies" distance | Continuous numeric data, default in kNN |
| **Manhattan** (L1) | $d(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | $p = 1$ | Sum of axis-aligned movements (city block) | High-dimensional sparse data |
| **Minkowski** (Generalized Lp) | $d(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ | $p \geq 1$ | Generalizes both L1 and L2 | Theoretical flexibility |
| **Chebyshev** (LŌł×) | $d(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \max_{j} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | $p \to \infty$ | Maximum coordinate-wise difference | King-move chess analogy, dominance distance |
| **Cosine Distance** | $d(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = 1 - \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert\mathbf{x}\Vert \, \Vert\mathbf{y}\Vert}$ | ŌĆö | Angular difference on unit sphere | Text / TF-IDF, document similarity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Hint:** For KTU exams, the **Minkowski** formula is the "parent" metric. Setting $p=1$ gives Manhattan; $p=2$ gives Euclidean. Examiners love asking: *"For what value of p does Minkowski distance reduce to Euclidean distance?"* ŌĆö Answer: **p = 2**.

## 2.4 The Bias-Variance Trade-off via k

The choice of $k$ is the single most important hyperparameter in kNN, and it directly governs the bias-variance trade-off.

- **k = 1 (very low)**: Maximum flexibility. The model fits the training data almost perfectly ŌĆö including its noise. **Low bias, high variance** (overfitting).
- **k = N (very high)**: Predicts the global majority class for every point. **High bias, low variance** (underfitting).
- **Optimal k (typically 3, 5, 7, 9)**: A balanced intermediate value, often selected by **k-fold cross-validation**.

$$
\text{Decision boundary smoothness} \;\propto\; k
$$

The larger the k, the smoother (and more linear-like) the decision boundary becomes.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

kNN is widely used in:

- **Recommendation systems** (e.g., suggesting similar products to a user).
- **Medical diagnosis** (classifying a tumor as malignant/benign from k closest historical cases).
- **Pattern recognition** (handwritten digit recognition ŌĆö MNIST).
- **Anomaly / outlier detection** (points with very large average distance to their k neighbors).
- **Missing-value imputation** (filling gaps by averaging the k nearest complete records).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Manual kNN Classification (KTU Classic Question)

**Problem Statement.** Consider the following 2-D training dataset, where each point belongs to one of two classes: **A (Apple)** or **B (Banana)**. A new fruit with features (X1, X2) = (2.5, 2.0) is to be classified using **kNN with k = 3** and the **Euclidean distance** metric.

| Point ID | Feature X1 (Sweetness) | Feature X2 (Crunchiness) | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 1.0 | 1.0 | A |
| P2 | 1.5 | 2.0 | A |
| P3 | 3.0 | 3.0 | B |
| P4 | 3.5 | 4.0 | B |
| P5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | A |

### Step 1 ŌĆö State the Query Point

$$
\mathbf{x}_q \;=\; (2.5,\; 2.0)
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Set Hyperparameters

$$
k \;=\; 3, \qquad \text{metric} \;=\; \text{Euclidean}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Euclidean Distances

The Euclidean distance formula for a 2-D case is:

$$
d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) \;=\; \sqrt{(x_{q,1} - x_{i,1})^{2} \;+\; (x_{q,2} - x_{i,2})^{2}}
$$

**Distance to P1 (1.0, 1.0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_1) &= \sqrt{(2.5 - 1.0)^{2} + (2.0 - 1.0)^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{(1.5)^{2} + (1.0)^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{2.25 + 1.00} \\
&= \sqrt{3.25} \\
&\approx 1.803
\end{aligned}
$$

**Distance to P2 (1.5, 2.0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_2) &= \sqrt{(2.5 - 1.5)^{2} + (2.0 - 2.0)^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{(1.0)^{2} + (0)^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{1.00} \\
&= 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Distance to P3 (3.0, 3.0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_3) &= \sqrt{(2.5 - 3.0)^{2} + (2.0 - 3.0)^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{(-0.5)^{2} + (-1.0)^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{0.25 + 1.00} \\
&= \sqrt{1.25} \\
&\approx 1.118
\end{aligned}
$$

**Distance to P4 (3.5, 4.0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_4) &= \sqrt{(2.5 - 3.5)^{2} + (2.0 - 4.0)^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{(-1.0)^{2} + (-2.0)^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{1.00 + 4.00} \\
&= \sqrt{5.00} \\
&\approx 2.236
\end{aligned}
$$

**Distance to P5 (2.0, 2.5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_5) &= \sqrt{(2.5 - 2.0)^{2} + (2.0 - 2.5)^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{(0.5)^{2} + (-0.5)^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25} \\
&= \sqrt{0.50} \\
&\approx 0.707
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Rank Distances in Ascending Order

$$
P_5 \;(0.707) \;\prec\; P_2 \;(1.000) \;\prec\; P_3 \;(1.118) \;\prec\; P_1 \;(1.803) \;\prec\; P_4 \;(2.236)
$$

The symbol "$\prec$" denotes "is closer than."

### Step 5 ŌĆö Identify the k = 3 Nearest Neighbors

$$
\mathcal{N}_3(\mathbf{x}_q) \;=\; \{ P_5, \, P_2, \, P_3 \}
$$

With their associated class labels:

$$
y_{P_5} = A, \qquad y_{P_2} = A, \qquad y_{P_3} = B
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Apply Majority Voting

The vote tally per class is:

$$
\text{Votes for A} \;=\; \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_3} \mathbb{1}(y_i = A) \;=\; \mathbb{1}(A) + \mathbb{1}(A) + \mathbb{1}(B) \;=\; 2
$$

$$
\text{Votes for B} \;=\; \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_3} \mathbb{1}(y_i = B) \;=\; 1
$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Final Prediction

$$
\hat{y}_q \;=\; \arg\max_{c \in \{A, B\}} \text{Votes}(c) \;=\; \arg\max(2, 1) \;=\; A
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The new fruit with features (2.5, 2.0) is classified as **Class A (Apple)** with 2 out of 3 votes.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö kNN Regression with Mean Aggregation

**Problem Statement.** Given the same five data points, suppose we wish to **predict the weight (y)** of a new point $\mathbf{x}_q = (2.5, 2.0)$ using **kNN regression with k = 3**. The target values are:

| Point | X1 | X2 | y (Weight) |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 50 g |
| P2 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 60 g |
| P3 | 3.0 | 3.0 | 80 g |
| P4 | 3.5 | 4.0 | 90 g |
| P5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 70 g |

The 3 nearest neighbors (from Example 3.1) are P5, P2, P3 with corresponding y-values 70, 60, 80.

### Apply the Regression Aggregation Rule

$$
\hat{y}_q \;=\; \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)} y_i
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_q &= \frac{1}{3} \,(y_{P_5} + y_{P_2} + y_{P_3}) \\
&= \frac{1}{3} \,(70 + 60 + 80) \\
&= \frac{1}{3} \times 210 \\
&= 70 \text{ grams}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The predicted weight of the new fruit is **70 g**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Distance-Weighted kNN

If we had used **distance-weighted** voting for the same Example 3.1, the calculation would become:

Weights $w_i = 1 / (d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_i) + \epsilon)$ using $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$:

$$
w_{P_5} = \frac{1}{0.707} \approx 1.414, \quad w_{P_2} = \frac{1}{1.000} = 1.000, \quad w_{P_3} = \frac{1}{1.118} \approx 0.894
$$

Weighted vote tally:

$$
\text{Score}(A) = w_{P_5} + w_{P_2} = 1.414 + 1.000 = 2.414
$$

$$
\text{Score}(B) = w_{P_3} = 0.894
$$

$$
\hat{y}_q = \arg\max(2.414, \, 0.894) = A
$$

The classification is unchanged, but the **margin of confidence** is now larger (2.414 vs 2 unweighted).

---

## 3.4 Decision Boundary Intuition ŌĆö Analytical Note

In 1-D binary classification with k = 1, the decision boundary is the **midpoint between adjacent points of opposite class**. For higher k, the boundary shifts and becomes smoother. This conceptual understanding is regularly tested in KTU Module 3 viva voce and 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the kNN Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Query Point xq] --> B[Load Training Dataset D]
    B --> C[Choose Hyperparameter k]
    C --> D[Choose Distance Metric]
    D --> E[Compute Distance d xq xi for all i]
    E --> F[Sort Distances Ascending]
    F --> G[Select Top k Training Points]
    G --> H{Task Type}
    H -- Classification --> I[Majority Voting or Weighted Voting]
    H -- Regression --> J[Arithmetic Mean or Weighted Mean]
    I --> K[Output Predicted Class y hat]
    J --> L[Output Predicted Value y hat]
    K --> M[End]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Feature-Space Neighbourhood Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Query Point xq] --- N1[Neighbor 1 Label A]
    Q --- N2[Neighbor 2 Label A]
    Q --- N3[Neighbor 3 Label B]
    Q -. Excluded .-> X1[Far Point Label A]
    Q -. Excluded .-> X2[Far Point Label B]
    classDef query fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef inside fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef outside fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    class Q query
    class N1,N2,N3 inside
    class X1,X2 outside
```

## 4.3 Decision Boundary as a Function of k (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1["k = 1: Highly Jagged Boundary"] --> K3["k = 3: Smoother Boundary"]
    K3 --> K5["k = 5: Even Smoother"]
    K5 --> K9["k = 9: Almost Linear"]
    K9 --> KN["k = N: Single Global Region"]
    classDef box fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    class K1,K3,K5,K9,KN box
```

## 4.4 Bias-Variance Trade-off Curve (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOWK["Small k: Low Bias / High Variance Overfitting"] --> MIDK["Moderate k: Optimal Generalization"]
    MIDK --> HIGHK["Large k: High Bias / Low Variance Underfitting"]
    classDef red fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef green fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef blue fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    class LOWK red
    class MIDK green
    class HIGHK blue
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid block diagrams above serve as a substitute for hand-drawn illustrations. They precisely capture the kNN pipeline, the in/out-of-neighborhood distinction, the boundary smoothness vs. k relationship, and the bias-variance trade-off ŌĆö all canonical KTU Module 3 visual expectations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Define the k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) algorithm. Why is it called a lazy learning algorithm?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** kNN is a non-parametric, instance-based supervised learning algorithm that classifies a query instance by the **majority class** of its **k nearest training examples** in the feature space, where "nearest" is defined by a distance metric (typically Euclidean).
- **Lazy Learning Justification (1.5 Marks):** kNN is called lazy because it **does not build any explicit model during training**; it simply memorizes the training dataset. All computation ŌĆö distance calculation, neighbor selection, voting ŌĆö is **deferred until a test query arrives**. There is no parameter estimation or generalization performed in the training phase, hence "lazy."

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three distance metrics used in kNN.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Euclidean Distance (L2 norm)** (1 Mark): The straight-line distance between two points in Euclidean space; $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_j - y_j)^2}$. Most common default metric.
2. **Manhattan Distance (L1 norm)** (1 Mark): The sum of absolute coordinate-wise differences; $d = \sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert$. Useful in high-dimensional sparse data.
3. **Minkowski Distance (Lp norm)** (1 Mark): A generalized metric $d = (\sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p)^{1/p}$ that reduces to Manhattan at $p=1$ and Euclidean at $p=2$.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **kNN classification algorithm** in detail, with its stepwise procedure. Discuss the role of the hyperparameter **k** and the choice of **distance metric**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** For the dataset given below, classify the query point $\mathbf{x}_q = (3.0, 4.0)$ using **kNN with k = 3** and **Euclidean distance**. *(7 Marks)*

| Point | X1 | X2 | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1 | 2 | C1 |
| B | 2 | 3 | C1 |
| C | 3 | 1 | C2 |
| D | 4 | 4 | C2 |
| E | 5 | 5 | C2 |

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Algorithm Explanation (7 Marks):**

The kNN classification algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. **Input**: Training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, query point $\mathbf{x}_q$, integer $k$, distance function $d(\cdot, \cdot)$. *(1 Mark)*
2. **Distance Computation**: For each $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{D}$, compute $d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)$. *(1 Mark)*
3. **Sorting**: Sort all computed distances in ascending order. *(0.5 Mark)*
4. **Neighbor Selection**: Select the k training points corresponding to the k smallest distances; this set is $\mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)$. *(1 Mark)*
5. **Majority Voting**: Assign $\hat{y}_q = \arg\max_c \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)$. *(1 Mark)*
6. **Role of k (1 Mark)**: A small k (e.g., 1) leads to overfitting (low bias, high variance). A large k leads to underfitting (high bias, low variance). The optimal k is chosen via cross-validation; odd k is preferred to avoid ties.
7. **Role of Distance Metric (1 Mark)**: Choice of metric (Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski) defines the notion of "closeness." Euclidean is most common for continuous data, Manhattan for high-dimensional/sparse data, Minkowski for general p-norm flexibility.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Classification (7 Marks):**

Step 1 ŌĆö Query point: $\mathbf{x}_q = (3.0, 4.0)$, $k = 3$.

Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Euclidean distances:

- $d(\mathbf{x}_q, A) = \sqrt{(3-1)^2 + (4-2)^2} = \sqrt{4+4} = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.828$
- $d(\mathbf{x}_q, B) = \sqrt{(3-2)^2 + (4-3)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$
- $d(\mathbf{x}_q, C) = \sqrt{(3-3)^2 + (4-1)^2} = \sqrt{0+9} = 3.000$
- $d(\mathbf{x}_q, D) = \sqrt{(3-4)^2 + (4-4)^2} = \sqrt{1+0} = 1.000$
- $d(\mathbf{x}_q, E) = \sqrt{(3-5)^2 + (4-5)^2} = \sqrt{4+1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$

Step 3 ŌĆö Sort distances ascending:
$D \;(1.000) \prec B \;(1.414) \prec E \;(2.236) \prec A \;(2.828) \prec C \;(3.000)$

Step 4 ŌĆö Top 3 nearest: **D, B, E** with classes **C2, C1, C2**.

Step 5 ŌĆö Majority voting:
- Votes for C1 = 1 (B)
- Votes for C2 = 2 (D, E)

Step 6 ŌĆö Prediction: $\hat{y}_q = C2$ **(Class 2)**.

---

### Question B (Alternative to Question A) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Sample Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss the **bias-variance trade-off** in kNN. How does the choice of k affect the model's performance and decision boundary? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Using **kNN regression with k = 3**, predict the value of y for the query point $\mathbf{x}_q = (4, 3)$. Use **Manhattan distance**. *(7 Marks)*

| Point | X1 | X2 | y |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 1 | 1 | 10 |
| P2 | 2 | 2 | 20 |
| P3 | 5 | 5 | 50 |
| P4 | 3 | 4 | 35 |
| P5 | 4 | 2 | 40 |

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Bias-Variance Trade-off (7 Marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Bias is the error from wrong assumptions; variance is the error from sensitivity to training-data fluctuations. kNN's bias-variance trade-off is controlled almost entirely by **k**.
- **Small k (e.g., k = 1) (1.5 Marks):** Model is highly sensitive to noise in individual training points. Decision boundary is **jagged and irregular**, with islands of single-class regions. **Low bias, high variance** ŌåÆ overfitting.
- **Large k (1.5 Marks):** Model becomes overly smooth. Decision boundary tends toward a single global majority class. **High bias, low variance** ŌåÆ underfitting.
- **Optimal k (1 Mark):** Typically 3, 5, or 7. Selected by k-fold cross-validation. Yields the lowest generalization error.
- **Decision boundary (1 Mark):** As k increases, the decision boundary transitions from a Voronoi-like mosaic (k=1) to a smooth, almost linear partition.
- **Practical tip (0.5 Mark):** Use odd k to avoid tie-breaks; in case of ties, use distance-weighted voting.

**Part (b) ŌĆö kNN Regression with Manhattan Distance (7 Marks):**

Step 1 ŌĆö Query: $\mathbf{x}_q = (4, 3)$, $k = 3$.

Step 2 ŌĆö Manhattan distance $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = |x_1 - y_1| + |x_2 - y_2|$:

- $d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_1) = |4-1| + |3-1| = 3 + 2 = 5$
- $d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_2) = |4-2| + |3-2| = 2 + 1 = 3$
- $d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_3) = |4-5| + |3-5| = 1 + 2 = 3$
- $d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_4) = |4-3| + |3-4| = 1 + 1 = 2$
- $d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_5) = |4-4| + |3-2| = 0 + 1 = 1$

Step 3 ŌĆö Sort ascending: $P_5 \;(1) \prec P_4 \;(2) \prec P_2 \;(3) \prec P_3 \;(3) \prec P_1 \;(5)$.

Step 4 ŌĆö Top 3 nearest: **P5, P4, P2** (tie at distance 3 broken alphabetically/arbitrarily to include P2 over P3). y-values: 40, 35, 20.

Step 5 ŌĆö Mean aggregation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_q &= \frac{1}{3}(y_{P_5} + y_{P_4} + y_{P_2}) \\
&= \frac{1}{3}(40 + 35 + 20) \\
&= \frac{95}{3} \\
&\approx 31.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Predicted value: $\hat{y}_q \approx 31.67$.**

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in kNN Questions (As per KTU Valuation Keys):**
> 1. **Forgetting the indicator function** in the majority-voting formula ŌĆö examiners expect the formal sum $\sum \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)$ rather than a verbal "count the votes." Loss: **0.5 ŌĆō 1 mark**.
> 2. **Not squaring** the coordinate differences in Euclidean distance. Writing $\sqrt{(x_1 - y_1) + (x_2 - y_2)}$ instead of $\sqrt{(x_1 - y_1)^2 + (x_2 - y_2)^2}$ is a **fatal error** ŌĆö full mark deduction.
> 3. **Failing to specify** whether k should be odd or even, or **ignoring the tie-breaking rule**. Always state that **odd k is preferred** in binary classification.
> 4. **Confusing the formula order**: Minkowski distance is $(\sum |x_j - y_j|^p)^{1/p}$, not $(\sum (x_j - y_j)^p)^{1/p}$ ŌĆö the absolute value is mandatory.
> 5. **Skipping the sorting step** in worked examples. Even if the top-3 are obvious, **show the sorted list** for full marks.
> 6. **Writing $k$ = 1 (rather than 3 or 5) by default** when the problem does not specify ŌĆö this indicates to the examiner that the student did not understand the role of k.
> 7. **Omitting the units / final answer statement** ŌĆö always end with a boxed/concluded line such as *"$\therefore$ the new point is classified as Class B."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for kNN (Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> - kNN = **K**-**N**earest **N**eighbors; non-parametric, instance-based, **supervised**, and **lazy**.
> - Used for **both classification (voting)** and **regression (averaging)**.
> - **Algorithm steps**: receive query ŌåÆ compute distances ŌåÆ sort ŌåÆ pick top k ŌåÆ aggregate ŌåÆ output prediction.
> - **Most-used distance metric**: Euclidean (L2). Alternatives: Manhattan (L1), Minkowski (Lp), Chebyshev (LŌł×), Cosine.
> - **Minkowski** is the parent metric; L1 Ōåö $p=1$, L2 Ōåö $p=2$.
> - **Choice of k**: small k ŌåÆ overfitting (low bias, high variance); large k ŌåÆ underfitting (high bias, low variance); optimal k is **odd** and chosen by **cross-validation**.
> - **Majority voting formula**: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c \sum \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)$.
> - **Regression formula**: $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{k}\sum y_i$.
> - **Distance-weighted voting** assigns $w_i = 1/(d_i + \epsilon)$ to mitigate the equal-weight bias.
> - **Time complexity**: $O(Nd)$ per query ŌĆö **slow at inference**, fast at training.
> - **Curse of dimensionality**: kNN degrades sharply when $d$ (number of features) is large; feature scaling (normalization) is **mandatory**.
> - **Feature normalization** (min-max or z-score) is **always required** before applying kNN to prevent one feature from dominating the distance.
> - **Pros**: simple, no training phase, naturally handles multi-class, no distribution assumption. **Cons**: slow at inference, memory-heavy, sensitive to irrelevant features and feature scale.
> - **Key application areas**: recommendation systems, medical diagnosis, OCR, anomaly detection, missing-value imputation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: k-Nearest Neighbors Implementation in R
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT722 - DATA SCIENCE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** k-Nearest Neighbors Implementation in R

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)** is a supervised, non-parametric, instance-based lazy learning algorithm used primarily for **classification** and, in its regression variant, for **regression** problems. Under the KTU 2024 *Data Science for Engineers* syllabus, it is classified as a **distance-based, geometry-driven algorithm** in which the class label of an unseen query instance is determined by a **majority vote** (mode) of the class labels of the **k closest training samples** in the feature space.

Formally, given a training set $T = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{n}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ and a query point $x_q$, the predicted class is:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \sum_{i \in N_k(x_q)} \mathbb{1}(y^{(i)} = c)$$

where $N_k(x_q)$ is the set of the *k* training points nearest to $x_q$ under a chosen distance metric, and $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** k-NN is the canonical example of a **Lazy Learner** ŌĆö it performs *no explicit model generalization* during training. All computation is deferred until prediction time. This is a frequently asked 3-mark definition in the KTU University Exam.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just moved to a new city and want to decide which restaurant to try for dinner. Instead of reading thousands of reviews, you ask your **5 closest friends** (k = 5) and go to the type of restaurant that **the majority of them recommend** ŌĆö say, 3 say "South Indian", 2 say "Chinese". You choose **South Indian**. That is *k-NN in plain English*.

The "closeness" of friends is the **distance metric** (how similar their taste is to yours, perhaps measured by cuisine preference scores), and the number of friends you poll is the **k hyperparameter**. The more similar your friends' taste profile is to yours (smaller distance), the more weight their vote effectively carries in determining your decision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Lazy"?** A lazy learner does not build a discriminant function $\hat{f}(x)$ during training. It simply *memorizes* the dataset. The "learning" is postponed until a query is made ŌĆö exactly the opposite of "eager" learners like Logistic Regression or Decision Trees.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Box

| Term | Meaning | KTU Board Note |
|---|---|---|
| **k** | Number of nearest neighbors polled | Odd values preferred for binary classification to avoid ties |
| **Distance Metric** | Function $d: \mathbb{R}^{d} \times \mathbb{R}^{d} \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski, Cosine |
| **Feature Space** | The d-dimensional vector space $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ in which data resides | Each axis = one predictor |
| **Voting (Classification)** | Mode of neighbor labels | Weighted by $1/d$ in advanced variants |
| **Averaging (Regression)** | Mean of neighbor target values | Optional distance-weighted |
| **Curse of Dimensionality** | Distance concentration in high $d$ | Critical pitfall in KTU viva |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Decomposition

The k-NN algorithm executes in the following sequence, and these exact steps are how KTU examiners expect the answer to be structured:

1. **Load the dataset** and split it into training set $D_{train}$ and test set $D_{test}$ (typical ratio 70:30 or 80:20).
2. **Normalize / Standardize** every numeric feature so that no single feature dominates the distance calculation. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **Min-Max scaling** and **Z-score standardization**.
3. **Choose the value of k** ŌĆö typically an odd integer in $\{1, 3, 5, 7, \dots, 21\}$.
4. **Choose the distance metric** ŌĆö usually **Euclidean** for continuous features.
5. **For each test instance $x_q \in D_{test}$**:
    - Compute the distance $d(x_q, x^{(i)})$ to *every* training instance $x^{(i)} \in D_{train}$.
    - Sort these distances in ascending order.
    - Select the first **k** entries ŌĆö this is the neighborhood $N_k(x_q)$.
    - Determine the predicted class $\hat{y}$ via majority vote.
6. **Evaluate** the predictions using a **Confusion Matrix**, and derive **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Step 2:** If the *age* feature ranges from 20ŌĆō70 and *salary* ranges from 20,000ŌĆō200,000, the salary axis will completely dominate the Euclidean distance. Normalization is **not optional** in KTU lab exams.

### 2.2 The "How" ŌĆö Detailed Mechanics of Distance

Distance metrics are mathematical functions that quantify dissimilarity between two vectors $x = (x_1, \dots, x_d)$ and $y = (y_1, \dots, y_d)$. The three required by the KTU 2024 OEITT722 syllabus are:

- **Euclidean** ($L_2$ norm) ŌĆö the geometric "straight-line" distance.
- **Manhattan** ($L_1$ norm) ŌĆö the city-block / taxicab distance.
- **Minkowski** ($L_p$ norm) ŌĆö the generalized family containing both above.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-grade reference material**. Memorize it for Part A and Part B derivations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Range / Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance ($L_2$) | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | $d \in [0, \infty)$, dimensionless | Default for continuous features |
| 2 | Manhattan Distance ($L_1$) | $d(x,y) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | $d \in [0, \infty)$, dimensionless | High-dim or sparse data |
| 3 | Minkowski Distance ($L_p$) | $d(x,y) = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^{p}\right)^{1/p}$ | $p \geq 1$ | Generalization of (1) and (2) |
| 4 | Min-Max Normalization | $x' = \dfrac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$ | $x' \in [0, 1]$ | Brings all features to common scale |
| 5 | Z-Score Standardization | $x' = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $x' \in \mathbb{R}$, mean=0, sd=1 | Preferred when outliers exist |
| 6 | k-NN Classification Rule | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{i \in N_k} \mathbb{1}(y^{(i)} = c)$ | Discrete class | Core prediction step |
| 7 | k-NN Regression Rule | $\hat{y} = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{i \in N_k} y^{(i)}$ | Continuous | Mean of neighbor targets |
| 8 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Overall correctness |
| 9 | Precision | $\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| 10 | Recall | $\text{Rec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| 11 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | $[0,1]$ | Harmonic mean balance |
| 12 | Error Rate | $\text{Err} = 1 - \text{Acc}$ | $[0,1]$ | Used in k-selection plots |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of k-NN

- **Healthcare:** Classifying tumors as malignant or benign from cell-image features.
- **Recommender Systems:** Finding users with similar purchase histories.
- **Credit Scoring:** Flagging loan applicants whose profile matches defaulters.
- **Pattern Recognition:** Handwritten digit classification (e.g., MNIST baseline).
- **Anomaly Detection:** Points whose k-th neighbor distance exceeds a threshold are anomalies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö Distance from First Principles

The Minkowski distance of order $p$ between two vectors $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d)$ and $y = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_d)$ is defined as the $p$-th root of the sum of the $p$-th power of absolute differences:

$$d_p(x,y) = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$$

We now derive the **Euclidean** and **Manhattan** forms as special cases.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Specialization to Euclidean ($p = 2$):**

Substitute $p = 2$ into the Minkowski formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_2(x,y) &= \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^{2} \right)^{1/2} \\
&= \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - y_j)^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

The absolute value is dropped because $(x_j - y_j)^2$ is always non-negative. This is the standard Pythagorean extension to $d$ dimensions.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Specialization to Manhattan ($p = 1$):**

Substitute $p = 1$ into the Minkowski formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_1(x,y) &= \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^{1} \right)^{1/1} \\
&= \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Limiting Case ($p \to \infty$, Chebyshev):**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_\infty(x,y) &= \lim_{p \to \infty} \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p} \\
&= \max_{j} \vert x_j - y_j \vert
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Chebyshev distance** and is the *fourth* member of the Lp family. While not in the KTU 2024 syllabus, knowing it earns bonus points in viva.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example ŌĆö Computing k-NN Prediction by Hand

This is the *exact* style of question KTU examiners set in Part B. Consider the following 1-D training data with classes $\{A, B\}$:

| $i$ | $x^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | A |
| 2 | 2 | A |
| 3 | 4 | B |
| 4 | 5 | B |
| 5 | 6 | B |

**Query point:** $x_q = 3$, with $k = 3$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Euclidean distances** (since $d = 1$, Euclidean = absolute value):

$$\begin{aligned}
d(3, 1) &= \vert 3 - 1 \vert = 2 \\
d(3, 2) &= \vert 3 - 2 \vert = 1 \\
d(3, 4) &= \vert 3 - 4 \vert = 1 \\
d(3, 5) &= \vert 3 - 5 \vert = 2 \\
d(3, 6) &= \vert 3 - 6 \vert = 3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sort in ascending order:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d(3, 2) &= 1, \quad y^{(2)} = A \\
d(3, 4) &= 1, \quad y^{(3)} = B \\
d(3, 1) &= 2, \quad y^{(1)} = A \\
d(3, 5) &= 2, \quad y^{(4)} = B \\
d(3, 6) &= 3, \quad y^{(5)} = B
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Select the k = 3 nearest neighbors:**

$$N_3(3) = \{(2, A), (4, B), (1, A)\}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Majority vote:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Count of A} &= 2 \\
\text{Count of B} &= 1 \\
\hat{y} &= \arg\max\{2, 1\} = A
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The query point $x_q = 3$ is classified as **Class A**.

### 3.3 Derivation of Min-Max Normalization

Given a feature column $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ with $x_{min} = \min(X)$ and $x_{max} = \max(X)$, we want to transform every value to the interval $[0, 1]$ via an affine map $x' = ax + b$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Boundary conditions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
x'_{min} &= a \cdot x_{min} + b = 0 \\
x'_{max} &= a \cdot x_{max} + b = 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Subtract the two equations:**

$$a \cdot (x_{max} - x_{min}) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{1}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for b:**

$$b = -a \cdot x_{min} = -\frac{x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final formula:**

$$x' = \frac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always fit the scaler on the **training data only**, then apply the *same* transformation to the test data. Using test-set statistics leaks information ŌĆö this is a **3-mark viva penalty**.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam Quick-Reference)

| Property | k-NN (k > 1) | k-NN (k = 1) | Logistic Regression | Decision Tree |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Learning Type | Lazy | Lazy | Eager | Eager |
| Training Cost | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(nd)$ | $O(nd \log n)$ |
| Prediction Cost | $O(nd)$ | $O(nd)$ | $O(d)$ | $O(\text{depth})$ |
| Parametric? | No | No | Yes | No |
| Handles Non-linearity | Yes | Yes | Limited | Yes |
| Sensitive to Scale? | **Yes (Critical)** | Yes | Less so | Less so |
| Interpretability | Low | Very Low | Medium | **High** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

The following diagram follows the Node Identifier Alpha Rule (alphanumeric IDs prefixed with letters, e.g., `nodeA`, `stepB`) and the Label Formatting Restriction (no markdown tags inside quoted labels ŌĆö only raw uppercase alphanumeric text).

### 4.2 End-to-End k-NN Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: RAW DATASET]) --> stepB[LOAD DATA INTO R DATAFRAME]
    stepB --> stepC{IS DATA CLEAN?}
    stepC -->|NO| stepD[HANDLE MISSING VALUES AND OUTLIERS]
    stepD --> stepE[REMOVE DUPLICATES]
    stepC -->|YES| stepF[PARTITION INTO TRAIN AND TEST]
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG[APPLY MIN MAX OR Z SCORE NORMALIZATION FIT ON TRAIN]
    stepG --> stepH[SELECT VALUE OF K USING CROSS VALIDATION]
    stepH --> stepI[CHOOSE DISTANCE METRIC EUCLIDEAN MANHATTAN OR MINKOWSKI]
    stepI --> stepJ[FOR EACH TEST POINT Q COMPUTE DISTANCE TO ALL TRAIN POINTS]
    stepJ --> stepK[SORT DISTANCES IN ASCENDING ORDER]
    stepK --> stepL[SELECT K NEAREST NEIGHBORS N K OF Q]
    stepL --> stepM[TAKE MAJORITY VOTE OF CLASS LABELS IN N K]
    stepM --> stepN[ASSIGN PREDICTED CLASS TO Q]
    stepN --> stepO[BUILD CONFUSION MATRIX AND COMPUTE ACCURACY PRECISION RECALL F1]
    stepO --> stepP{IS ACCURACY SATISFACTORY?}
    stepP -->|NO| stepH
    stepP -->|YES| stepQ[TUNE FINAL MODEL AND DEPLOY]
    stepQ --> endR([END: CLASSIFIER READY])
```

### 4.3 Distance Metric Selection Flow (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([BEGIN: DATA READY]) --> qA{ARE FEATURES CONTINUOUS?}
    qA -->|YES| qB{IS DIMENSIONALITY GREATER THAN 20?}
    qA -->|NO| qC[USE HAMMING OR JACCARD DISTANCE]
    qB -->|NO| qD[USE EUCLIDEAN L2]
    qB -->|YES| qE{ARE OUTLIERS PRESENT?}
    qE -->|YES| qF[USE MANHATTAN L1]
    qE -->|NO| qG[USE COSINE DISTANCE]
    qD --> endR([END: METRIC FIXED])
    qF --> endR
    qG --> endR
    qC --> endR
```

### 4.4 Decision Boundary Intuition (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classA[CLASS A REGION]:::green
    classB[CLASS B REGION]:::blue
    queryQ((QUERY POINT Q)):::yellow
    n1((N1)):::red
    n2((N2)):::red
    n3((N3)):::red
    n4((N4)):::red
    n5((N5)):::red
    queryQ --- n1
    queryQ --- n2
    queryQ --- n3
    queryQ --- n4
    queryQ --- n5
    classA -. boundary .-> classB
    classDef green fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef blue fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef yellow fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef red fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The yellow node `queryQ` is the unknown test point. The five red nodes `n1` through `n5` are its k = 5 nearest neighbors in feature space. The k-NN rule will assign `queryQ` to whichever colored class region (green A or blue B) contains the majority of its neighbors.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  
> **Define the k-Nearest Neighbors algorithm. Why is it called a *lazy learner*?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
k-NN is a non-parametric, supervised classification algorithm that predicts the class of a query instance by a majority vote among its *k* closest training examples in the feature space, where closeness is measured by a distance metric (typically Euclidean).  
It is called a *lazy learner* because it performs **no explicit model construction during the training phase**; it simply stores the training data. All the computational work ŌĆö distance calculations, sorting, voting ŌĆö is deferred until a query is made at prediction time. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for non-parametric/supervision, 1 mark for lazy learner explanation.)

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`  
> **List any three distance metrics used in k-NN and write their mathematical formulas.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Euclidean distance** ($L_2$): $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - y_j)^2}$
- **Manhattan distance** ($L_1$): $d(x,y) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$
- **Minkowski distance** ($L_p$): $d(x,y) = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$, $p \geq 1$

(1 mark per correct formula. Always include the dimension $d$.)

---

### 5.2 PART B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Q.A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  
> **(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure of the k-Nearest Neighbors algorithm. Discuss the role of feature normalization and choice of $k$ in determining the model's performance. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Implement the k-NN algorithm in R to classify the *iris* dataset (use only `Sepal.Length`, `Sepal.Width`, `Petal.Length`, `Petal.Width` as features). Use $k = 5$ and a 70:30 train-test split. Display the confusion matrix and compute the accuracy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
    1. Load and split the dataset into training and test sets.
    2. Normalize all numeric features (e.g., Min-Max or Z-score).
    3. Choose the value of $k$ and the distance metric (Euclidean default).
    4. For each test instance $x_q$, compute the distance to all training points.
    5. Sort distances in ascending order; select the $k$ smallest.
    6. Determine the predicted class via majority vote among the $k$ labels.

2. **Role of Feature Normalization (2 marks):**  
   If features are on different scales, the feature with the largest variance will dominate the Euclidean distance, leading to biased neighbor selection. Normalization rescales every feature to a common range (e.g., $[0,1]$ via Min-Max), ensuring equal contribution to the distance metric. Fit the scaler on training data only to avoid data leakage.

3. **Choice of $k$ (2 marks):**  
   - Small $k$ (e.g., 1) ŌåÆ low bias, high variance, sensitive to noise (overfitting).  
   - Large $k$ ŌåÆ high bias, low variance, may underfit (over-smoothed decision boundary).  
   - **Practical rule:** Use odd $k$ for binary classification, and select optimal $k$ via cross-validation.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 marks] ŌĆö R Implementation

```r
# Step 1 ŌĆö Load the required library
library(class)
library(caret)

# Step 2 ŌĆö Load and prepare the iris dataset
data(iris)
df <- iris[, 1:4]             # Features: Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width, Petal.Length, Petal.Width
label <- iris$Species         # Target class

# Step 3 ŌĆö Normalize the features using Min-Max scaling
normalize <- function(x) {
  return((x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)))
}
df_norm <- as.data.frame(lapply(df, normalize))

# Step 4 ŌĆö Train-test split (70:30) with a fixed random seed for reproducibility
set.seed(123)
train_index <- sample(1:nrow(df_norm), 0.7 * nrow(df_norm))
train_x <- df_norm[train_index, ]
test_x  <- df_norm[-train_index, ]
train_y <- label[train_index]
test_y  <- label[-train_index]

# Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the k-NN classifier with k = 5
pred <- knn(train = train_x, test = test_x, cl = train_y, k = 5)

# Step 6 ŌĆö Confusion matrix
conf_mat <- table(Predicted = pred, Actual = test_y)
print(conf_mat)

# Step 7 ŌĆö Compute accuracy
accuracy <- sum(diag(conf_mat)) / sum(conf_mat)
cat("Accuracy =", round(accuracy * 100, 2), "%\n")
```

**Expected Output (typical run):**

```
            Actual
Predicted    setosa versicolor virginica
  setosa         15          0         0
  versicolor      0         14         1
  virginica       0          1        14
Accuracy = 95.56 %
```

> **Mark Distribution:** (1 mark) library loading, (1 mark) normalization, (1 mark) train-test split, (1 mark) `knn()` call, (1 mark) confusion matrix, (1 mark) accuracy computation, (1 mark) correct output interpretation.

---

> **Q.B)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`  
> **(a)** Derive the Euclidean and Manhattan distance formulas as special cases of the Minkowski distance. Compute, by hand, the Euclidean distance between $x = (1, 2, 3)$ and $y = (4, 6, 8)$. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Explain how the *optimal* value of $k$ is selected using 10-fold cross-validation in R. Write the R code to plot the misclassification error rate against different values of $k$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 marks]

**Derivation (4 marks):**  
Starting from the Minkowski definition:
$$d_p(x,y) = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$$

- **Substitute $p = 2$:** $d_2(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - y_j)^2}$ ŌĆö Euclidean.
- **Substitute $p = 1$:** $d_1(x,y) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ ŌĆö Manhattan.

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**  
Given $x = (1, 2, 3)$ and $y = (4, 6, 8)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_2(x,y) &= \sqrt{(1-4)^2 + (2-6)^2 + (3-8)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(-3)^2 + (-4)^2 + (-5)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{9 + 16 + 25} \\
&= \sqrt{50} \\
&= 5\sqrt{2} \approx 7.0711
\end{aligned}$$

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 marks] ŌĆö R Code

```r
# Step 1 ŌĆö Load libraries
library(class)
library(caret)

# Step 2 ŌĆö Prepare data
data(iris)
df_norm <- as.data.frame(lapply(iris[, 1:4], function(x) (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))))
label <- iris$Species

# Step 3 ŌĆö Define 10-fold cross-validation
set.seed(42)
folds <- createFolds(label, k = 10, list = TRUE)

# Step 4 ŌĆö Try k from 1 to 25 and compute mean CV error
k_values <- 1:25
cv_error <- sapply(k_values, function(k) {
  err <- sapply(folds, function(idx) {
    train_x <- df_norm[-idx, ]
    test_x  <- df_norm[idx, ]
    train_y <- label[-idx]
    test_y  <- label[idx]
    pred <- knn(train_x, test_x, train_y, k = k)
    mean(pred != test_y)
  })
  mean(err)
})

# Step 5 ŌĆö Plot the CV error vs k
plot(k_values, cv_error, type = "b", pch = 19, col = "blue",
     xlab = "Value of k", ylab = "Misclassification Error",
     main = "10-Fold CV: Choosing Optimal k for k-NN")

# Step 6 ŌĆö Identify the optimal k
optimal_k <- k_values[which.min(cv_error)]
cat("Optimal k =", optimal_k, "with error =", min(cv_error), "\n")
```

> **Mark Distribution:** (2 marks) correct k-selection theory, (2 marks) `createFolds()` usage, (2 marks) plotting logic, (1 mark) optimal-k identification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize** ŌåÆ loses 2 marks immediately. Always scale before `knn()`.
> 2. **Using test-set statistics for scaling** ŌåÆ data leakage, lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Choosing even $k$ in binary classification** ŌåÆ tie-vote ambiguity, lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing accuracy with F1-score** in imbalanced datasets ŌåÆ 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not setting a random seed** ŌåÆ results are non-reproducible; lab exams deduct 1 mark.
> 6. **Using `as.factor()` inconsistently** for class labels ŌåÆ 1-mark deduction in confusion matrix.
> 7. **Forgetting to load `library(class)`** ŌåÆ code will not run, lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember
